splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on when actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability under ERISA must be finalized. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be finalized before the end of the prior plan year, providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, extending the court's authority to revoke supervised release beyond the originally specified term.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that courts lack authority to toll supervised release absent explicit statutory authorization, and the term expires as specified in the judgment regardless of absconding.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face the additional gatekeeping requirement of Section 2244(b)(1) — which categorically bars claims previously presented — when bringing successive collateral attacks under Section 2255, or whether they need only satisfy 2255(h)'s separate requirements.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, in addition to § 2255(h)'s requirements.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, meaning they need only satisfy the requirements of § 2255(h).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to challenge election administration rules before an election takes place. The resolution affects how easily pre-election challenges to voting procedures can be brought nationwide, with implications for mail-in voting rules, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate for federal office suffers a cognizable competitive injury sufficient to establish Article III standing to challenge election rules that may affect ballot counting.

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's speculative and generalized claims of competitive harm from election administration rules do not satisfy Article III's injury-in-fact requirement.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps Auer/Kisor deference to agency interpretations in criminal sentencing contexts, specifically regarding Sentencing Guidelines commentary.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity should prevail over deference to agency interpretations (including Sentencing Commission commentary) when construing ambiguous provisions that carry criminal consequences. The Third Circuit held deference trumps lenity, while the Fourth, Fifth, and Eleventh Circuits hold that lenity prevails.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over Auer/Kisor deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretation.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference (Auer/Kisor) prevails over the rule of lenity when interpreting Sentencing Guidelines commentary, allowing the Sentencing Commission's anti-defendant reading to control.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief under the First Step Act only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria or if possessing any one is disqualifying. This determines whether district courts must impose sentences without regard to statutory minimums, directly affecting defendants' liberty.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is disqualifying, reading 'and' as 'or' based on statutory context and structure.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses is a valid, authoritative interpretation deserving deference under Stinson/Kisor. This affects whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career offenders.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 reasonably interprets genuinely ambiguous guideline text and is entitled to controlling weight under Stinson and Kisor, so conspiracy qualifies as a controlled substance offense.

positions.label: Commentary is not entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and the commentary impermissibly expands the definition, so it should not receive deference under Kisor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to § 924(c)) can constitute or contribute to an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are sharply divided on whether non-retroactive changes in law can serve as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically prohibit it, while others allow it as one factor among several. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to permit it, but some circuits have held the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in law, alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A), as this would circumvent Congress's decision not to make the change retroactive.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law may be considered as one factor among others in evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release, consistent with the Sentencing Commission's 2024 policy statement.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state law covers a substance not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally scheduled, or whether any state-scheduled substance suffices. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State law controls — no federal schedule limitation
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include any 'offense under state law' without requiring that the particular substance be federally scheduled.

positions.label: Federal schedule required
positions.summary: Holds that a prior state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must bring challenges to the conditions or execution of their confinement (such as BOP decisions on earned time credits or prerelease custody) exclusively through habeas corpus, or whether they may also use civil rights actions (e.g., Bivens or APA).
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions affecting the terms of their confinement, or whether civil rights and statutory remedies remain available. This affects how prisoners challenge First Step Act time-credit calculations and transfer decisions.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for duration-of-confinement challenges
positions.summary: Holds that challenges affecting the fact or duration of confinement, including BOP decisions on earned time credits and prerelease custody, must be brought through habeas corpus and cannot proceed as civil rights actions.

positions.label: Civil rights actions also available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners challenging conditions or execution of their sentences may proceed through civil rights or statutory actions and are not limited to habeas corpus, especially where the challenge does not directly seek release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when revoking and resentencing, or whether revocation sentences may only be based on the need for sanction and deterrence related to the violation.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether sentences imposed upon revocation of supervised release may be expressly punitive. The Sixth Circuit permits courts to expressly punish defendants for violations, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split, while other circuits hold that revocation sentencing should focus on the breach of trust rather than punishment for the underlying violation conduct.

positions.label: Courts may expressly punish for violations
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may impose revocation sentences that expressly punish defendants for their violations of supervised release conditions.

positions.label: Courts may not impose punishment for violation conduct
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should be based on the breach of the court's trust, not on punishing the defendant for the conduct underlying the violation, limiting the permissible considerations at revocation sentencing.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the time limit to appeal an order on a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is the 30-day civil deadline or the 14-day criminal deadline.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (triggering the 30-day civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case itself (triggering the 14-day criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline (30 days) applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Manchester Farming Partnership

positions.label: Criminal deadline (14 days) applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises from and is part of the criminal case, so the shorter criminal appeal deadline applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in property equity belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held appreciation belongs to the estate, creating a split with circuits holding it belongs to the debtor.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the broad scope of 'property of the estate' under § 541(a) means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion, and § 348(f)(1)(A) does not change this result.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that under § 348(f)(1)(A), property of the estate upon conversion is valued as of the original filing date, meaning post-petition appreciation in equity remains with the good-faith converting debtor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as a matter implicating a value of 'high order.'
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that medical privacy constitutes a 'high order' value warranting immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine when a motion to seal medical records is denied. This approach may conflict with other circuits' more restrictive application of the collateral order doctrine to sealing orders.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine when a motion to seal is denied.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denials of motions to seal are not the type of order subject to immediate appeal under the collateral order doctrine, as the right to seal can be adequately vindicated by other means or on final appeal.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as a basis (alone or in combination with other factors) for compassionate release. Several circuits hold that such changes cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others permit courts to consider them. The dispute has been further complicated by the Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive sentencing law changes, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot constitute extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as one factor among others in evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to' broker services, preempts common-law negligence claims against freight brokers for injuries arising from their selection of motor carriers. The split has major implications for the trucking industry and accident victims' ability to recover damages.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted by the FAAAA because such claims relate to the broker's services in arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that FAAAA does not preempt common-law negligence claims against freight brokers because general tort duties are not the type of state regulation Congress intended to preempt.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Aspen American Insurance Co. v. Landstar Ranger, Inc.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus relief — such as alleged legal errors in the underlying conviction — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under the compassionate release statute. Some circuits permit consideration while others categorically bar it.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts have discretion to consider a broad range of factors, including potential legal errors in the conviction or sentence, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for challenging the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be raised through habeas corpus under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 or related civil rights statutes?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to discrimination claims in hospitality settings, indicating a circuit split on the applicable legal standards for such claims. The case will clarify standards for fair treatment, discrimination liability, and damages in service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: Applied a particular legal framework to discrimination claims arising from hospitality establishments that the Supreme Court found sufficiently unsettled to warrant review.

positions.label: Differing approach by other circuits
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards to discrimination claims in commercial or hospitality contexts, creating the split that prompted SCOTUS review.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in New York State Rifle & Pistol Ass'n v. Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances, particularly marijuana, is consistent with the Second Amendment's text and the nation's historical tradition of firearms regulation.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that the historical tradition of disarming those who pose a danger to themselves or others, including habitual substance users, supports the constitutionality of § 922(g)(3) even after Bruen.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government cannot demonstrate a sufficient historical analogue for disarming non-violent drug users.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the scope of the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'engaged in . . . interstate commerce.' This affects the enforceability of arbitration agreements for a wide range of distribution workers.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are part of a continuous interstate transportation chain, including those making last-mile deliveries of goods that have moved in interstate commerce, qualify as transportation workers exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that distribution workers delivering goods locally to retail stores are not 'transportation workers engaged in . . . interstate commerce' under Section 1 because they are not themselves moving goods across state lines.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state-law failure-to-warn claims against pesticide manufacturers when the claims are based on labeling that the EPA approved. This affects product liability litigation involving agricultural chemicals and consumer pesticides nationwide.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling different from or in addition to what the EPA has approved.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims because they impose requirements 'parallel to' rather than 'in addition to' FIFRA's labeling requirements, consistent with the Supreme Court's Bates v. Dow Agrosciences framework.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
